0:12.rendering: I set my heart to explore everything done under the sun, using wisdom as my guide — and I have learned this is a heavy burden God has laid on us all.
0:12.note: The Hebrew word for "sore travail" pictures a man straining under a load too heavy to carry alone, which is exactly the point — Solomon tried wisdom as his savior and found it exhausting, because only Jesus is strong enough to carry what we cannot.

0:0.rendering: "These are the collected words of the Preacher — a son of David, a king who once ruled Jerusalem."
0:0.note: The Hebrew title Qohelet means someone who gathers people together to speak, and Jesus is the ultimate fulfillment of that — the one who calls us to himself, opens his mouth, and gives us words that actually satisfy the emptiness this whole book is about to describe.

0:1.rendering: "Smoke and mirrors," says the Teacher, "smoke and mirrors — nothing here will last."
0:1.note: The Hebrew word hebel literally means "breath" or "vapor" — something real but gone the moment you grasp it, which is exactly why Jesus said follow me, not your achievements.

0:2.rendering: "What do we really gain from all our striving beneath this sun?"
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "profit" comes from merchant life — the surplus left after a trade — and Solomon is asking whether any of our exhausting deals with this world leave us with anything that actually lasts; Jesus answers that question directly when he says a man can gain the whole world and still lose everything that matters.

0:3.rendering: Generations come and go, but the earth stands firm forever.
0:3.note: Qoheleth uses the same Hebrew word for "passing away" that describes a river rushing past — here today, gone tomorrow — while the earth just stays, immovable and patient, the way God's faithfulness stays beneath every restless human life. Jesus, the one who made that earth and walked on it, is the only solid ground worth standing on.

0:4.rendering: The sun rises and the sun sets, rushing back to where it will rise again.
0:4.note: The Hebrew word for "hastens" carries the feeling of breathless longing — as if even creation itself can't wait to begin again, pointing us to the God who makes all things new.

0:5.rendering: The wind spirals south, then swings back north, round and round in its endless circuits, always returning to where it began.
0:5.note: Every ancient version of this text — Hebrew, Greek, Latin — reaches for the same image: not just wind moving, but wind returning, caught in a loop it cannot break free from, which is exactly Solomon's point and exactly why we need a Savior who breaks cycles rather than repeating them.

0:6.rendering: All the rivers pour into the sea, yet the sea is never filled; and to the place where the rivers begin, there they return to flow again.
0:6.note: The Preacher isn't teaching hydrology — he's showing us that creation itself is caught in a loop it cannot escape, longing for a fullness only Jesus, the Living Water, can finally provide.

0:7.rendering: Everything exhausts itself trying; no words can hold it all — the eye keeps craving more to see, the ear more to hear.
0:7.note: Qohelet isn't complaining about boredom — he's diagnosing a hunger built into us that the world simply cannot feed, and Jesus meets us exactly there, saying I am the bread, I am the living water, I am enough.

0:8.rendering: What has happened before will happen again; what has been done before will be done again — there is nothing truly new under the sun.
0:8.note: Solomon isn't crushing your dreams — he's freeing you from the exhausting pressure to be original, because the only genuinely new thing in history was an empty tomb, and that changes everything.

0:9.rendering: "Is there anything that can truly be called new? It was here long before any of us arrived."
0:9.note: The Preacher isn't crushing curiosity — he's freeing us from the exhausting pressure to be original, because the only genuinely new thing in history was an empty tomb on Sunday morning.

0:10.rendering: "Nothing from the past is remembered, and nothing happening now will be remembered by those who come after."
0:10.note: The Hebrew word often translated "remembrance" carries the weight of being known and kept — the way a shepherd knows his sheep by name. Solomon's ache here is the very wound Jesus came to heal: you are not forgotten. He calls you by name, and his memory of you will never fade.

0:11.rendering: "I, the Gatherer, held the throne over God's people in Jerusalem."
0:11.note: The Hebrew word Qohelet means someone who pulls people together — a convener, a collector of wisdom — and Jesus is the ultimate fulfillment of that role, the one who gathers the scattered and lost into one family around himself.

1:11.rendering: "So I turned to take a good look at wisdom, madness, and folly — because what can anyone who comes after a king actually do that hasn't been done before?"
1:11.note: Solomon had every resource to chase meaning, and still hit a wall — which is exactly why Jesus, the wiser-than-Solomon king, doesn't just show us the way through the wall, he is the way through it.

1:12.rendering: I saw it clearly: wisdom outshines folly the way daylight swallows darkness whole.
1:12.note: Every translator has to choose between "excels" and "outshines" — I chose light language because Solomon isn't making an argument, he's having a moment of seeing, and Jesus himself said "I am the light," so when wisdom finally wins, it wins the way dawn wins: not by defeating the dark but by simply arriving.

1:13.rendering: "The wise person keeps their eyes open, but the fool stumbles through life in the dark — yet in the end, death comes for them both."
1:13.note: Solomon isn't celebrating wisdom here, he's mourning its limits — because no amount of seeing clearly changes the fact that we all die, which is exactly why we need someone who walked out of the grave.

1:14.rendering: "So I thought to myself: the fool and I share the same fate — so what did all my wisdom actually get me? And I concluded: this too is empty."
1:14.note: Qohelet doesn't soften the pain here — he laughs bitterly at his own résumé, and that honest grief is exactly where Jesus meets us, because he's the only wise man whose wisdom actually outlasted death.

1:15.rendering: There is no lasting memory of the wise any more than the fool — both will be forgotten. And the wise man? He dies just like the fool.
1:15.note: The Hebrew word for "remembrance" carries the weight of being known — and Solomon's raw honesty here isn't despair, it's a door: if no human legacy survives, then only what is hidden in Christ truly lasts.

1:16.rendering: So I came to hate my life, because everything done here under the sun felt like a burden to me — hollow, like chasing the wind.
1:16.note: Qohelet's word for "vanity" is hebel, a breath, a vapor — something you can see for a moment but never hold, which is exactly why only Jesus, the Word made flesh, gives us something solid enough to actually grip.

1:17.rendering: So I came to hate everything I had worked so hard for under the sun, knowing I would have to leave it all to someone who comes after me.
1:17.note: The Preacher's grief isn't laziness — it's the ache of a man who built his whole life on sand, and Jesus is the only inheritance that no death, no heir, and no grave can ever take away from you.

1:18.rendering: "And who can know if the one who inherits everything I built with such wisdom will turn out to be a fool? That too is a breath of smoke."
1:18.note: The Hebrew word hebel — usually rendered "vanity" — literally means a puff of vapor, a wisp of breath, which is why Jesus said don't store up treasure on earth; your heir might blow it all before the week is out.

1:19.rendering: "So I turned my heart to give up on everything I had worked so hard for under the sun."
1:19.note: The Hebrew word here carries the weight of a man who doesn't just feel disappointed but actually decides to let go — and that surrendered grip is exactly where Jesus meets us.

1:0.rendering: "I told myself, 'Come on now, let's try pleasure and see what happiness really tastes like.' But that, too, turned out to be empty."
1:0.note: The Preacher wasn't guessing from a distance — he dove headfirst into every joy the world offered, only to surface gasping in shallowness, which is exactly why Jesus calls himself Living Water rather than a deeper pool of the same.

1:1.rendering: "I said about laughter, 'You're just noise,' and about celebration, 'What does any of this actually get you?'"
1:1.note: The Hebrew word for "mad" here isn't anger — it's the hollow spin of a wheel going nowhere, and Jesus is the one who finally gives the wheel somewhere to go.

1:2.rendering: I explored with my whole heart how to cheer myself with wine while still holding on to wisdom — testing out foolishness too — until I could figure out what is actually worth doing during the brief time we have on this earth.
1:2.note: Qohelet wasn't going off the rails; he was a man desperately running every experiment life offers, hoping pleasure or folly would finally satisfy — and the whole book is God's mercy in letting us read his lab notes so we don't have to repeat them, because Jesus is the answer Qohelet never quite had the name for.

1:3.rendering: "I launched grand projects—built myself palaces, planted myself vineyards—"
1:3.note: Qohelet stacks these achievements like trophies on a shelf, but the repeated "myself, myself, myself" is the whole point: a life curved inward on itself is already its own prison, and Jesus invites us into the only life curved outward enough to finally be free.

1:4.rendering: I planted gardens and parks for myself, filling them with every kind of fruit tree.
1:4.note: Qohelet builds an empire of beauty with his own hands — notice he says "for myself" three times in this chapter, which is precisely the problem; the man who has everything but lives for himself is the man who has nothing, and only Jesus, who had everything yet gave it all away, shows us what a life truly well-planted looks like.

1:5.rendering: "I built reservoirs to water the groves of growing trees."
1:5.note: Qohelet is showing us how even the most breathtaking human projects — reshaping land, coaxing life from the ground — leave the soul dry, because we were made to drink from a Spring we cannot engineer.

1:6.rendering: I acquired servants and slave-girls — some bought, some born right in my household — and my herds and flocks were larger than anyone before me had ever managed in Jerusalem.
1:6.note: Qohelet lists his assets like a man reading off a balance sheet, and that's exactly the point — when you reduce people to line items and livestock to net worth, you've already lost something no ledger can recover, which is why Jesus calls us to a kingdom where the last are first and no one is inventory.

1:7.rendering: I accumulated silver and gold for myself, and the private treasuries of kings and distant lands; I gathered choirs of men and women, and every pleasure the human heart craves — concubines beyond counting.
1:7.note: That last phrase, softened for centuries into "musical instruments," is almost certainly a euphemism for a royal harem — Solomon is confessing that he tried to fill his soul with everything, and it still came up empty, which is exactly the hole only Jesus can fill.

1:8.rendering: I grew greater than anyone who had ruled Jerusalem before me — and through it all, my wisdom never left me.
1:8.note: The Preacher isn't bragging; he's building a case — "I had everything, and it still wasn't enough" — which is exactly why only Jesus, the greater Solomon, can satisfy what Solomon himself could not find.

1:9.rendering: "I refused my eyes nothing they wanted, and I let my heart enjoy every pleasure — for my heart took delight in all my work, and this enjoyment was the reward for all my labor."
1:9.note: Qohelet kept a meticulous ledger of every pleasure he could buy, yet the Hebrew word for "portion" — heleq — quietly echoes like a warning bell, because in the Old Testament a person's true portion is not what they grab but what God gives; Jesus would later say the same thing to a man who wanted more inheritance money: "your life does not consist in the abundance of your possessions" — so what looks like a celebration in this verse is actually Solomon slowly building his own prosecution.

1:10.rendering: "I stepped back and looked at everything I had worked so hard to build—and it was all hollow. Chasing the wind. Nothing gained under the sun."
1:10.note: Solomon had wealth, wisdom, and power that no one before or since has matched, yet he still ended up empty-handed—which means the answer was never more of what he already had more of than anyone. Jesus didn't come to help you build a better pile; he came to offer you the one thing that actually lasts.

1:20.rendering: For there is a man who labors with wisdom, knowledge, and skill, yet must hand over everything he has built to someone who never lifted a finger for it. This too is vanity and a great evil.
1:20.note: The Hebrew word "hevel" — vapor, breath, mist — echoes through this whole book, and here it carries a particular sting: you can pour your whole life into something and then be forced to give it to someone who did nothing to earn it. Solomon is hitting us where it hurts so we'll stop gripping our résumés and our portfolios and turn our hands open toward Jesus, the only inheritance that nobody can take from us and nobody else will ever receive in our place.

1:21.rendering: "What does a person really gain from all their hard work and anxious striving under the sun?"
1:21.note: The Hebrew word for "striving" carries the image of someone chasing wind — and Jesus said the same thing differently: Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened. Qohelet diagnoses the disease; Jesus offers the cure.

1:22.rendering: For all his days are full of pain, and his work is a source of frustration — even at night his mind finds no rest. This too is pointless.
1:22.note: The Hebrew word translated "vexation" or "grief" carries the sense of anger turned inward — the exhausted person who can't even enjoy sleep because their mind keeps relitigating the day. Solomon is describing the hamster wheel of life lived for achievement rather than God, and Jesus explicitly invites everyone stuck on that wheel to come to him and finally rest (Matthew 11:28).

1:23.rendering: There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find satisfaction in their work. This too, I see, is from the hand of God.
1:23.note: The Preacher has been chasing meaning everywhere else and keeps coming up empty — then lands here, almost surprised: the simple pleasures aren't a consolation prize, they're a gift God presses into your hands. Jesus said he came so we might have life and have it to the full, and this is what full looks like on a Tuesday — a good meal, honest work, a moment of quiet gratitude.

1:24.rendering: "For who can eat or find enjoyment apart from him?"
1:24.note: The Hebrew here asks a question that answers itself — no one can truly savor life while trying to cut God out of the equation, and Jesus, the bread of life, is the one in whom every good gift finally makes sense.

1:25.rendering: To the one who pleases him, God gives wisdom, knowledge, and joy — but to the sinner, he gives the miserable task of hoarding and scraping, only to hand it all over to the one who pleases God. This too is empty. This too is chasing wind.
1:25.note: The word translated "pleases" in Hebrew carries the idea of being good in God's sight — not moral perfection, but a right-standing relationship. Solomon is describing what we now call grace: the person who rests in God receives gifts freely, while the person running from God exhausts themselves building a pile they can't keep. Jesus is the one who perfectly pleased the Father, and in him, we become the ones grace runs toward.

0:16.rendering: And I gave my heart to know wisdom, and to know madness and folly — and I came to see that this too is a chasing after wind.
0:16.note: The Preacher tried the oldest human shortcut: maybe if I just understand my emptiness, it will stop being empty — but wisdom about meaninglessness is still meaninglessness, and Jesus doesn't offer a better philosophy, he offers himself.

0:17.rendering: For in much wisdom is much grief, and whoever increases knowledge increases sorrow.
0:17.note: The Hebrew word for "grief" here carries the sense of a vexed, irritated pain — like a wound that won't stop throbbing. Solomon had seen everything under the sun and it only made the ache worse, because more knowledge without the Knower just means a bigger map of a broken world. Jesus doesn't give us less knowledge — he gives us himself as the destination, which finally makes the knowing worth the pain.
